data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2035 Lifetime Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The first quarter marked a reversal of the trends in place during 2024, with a poor showing for U.S. growth stocks but stronger returns for most other asset categories.
  • The John Hancock Multimanager 2035 Lifetime Portfolio fund (MUTF:JLHAX) underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation detracted from results, as did the relative performance of the fund's underlying managers.

Market review and outlook

Uncertainty regarding U.S. tariffs led to a wide range of returns across the world financial markets in the first quarter. U.S. equities were one of the worst performing asset categories on concerns that Washington's trade policies could fuel resurgent inflation and depress economic growth. Additionally, worries about reduced investment in AI-related infrastructure caused a sell-off in U.S. technology stocks. Although the U.S. growth style was hit hard by these developments, the value category posted a gain as investors sought opportunities in areas that had underperformed in 2024. Developed- and emerging-market stocks also recorded positive returns at a time of U.S. dollar weakness and positive sentiment on the outlook for European fiscal spending.

In the fixed-income market, higher-quality and more interest rate-sensitive assets delivered solid gains at a time of falling inflation and renewed hopes that the U.S. Federal Reserve would ease policy later in the year. The credit sectors (including investment-grade corporates, high-yield bonds, bank loans, and the emerging markets) produced positive returns but didn't keep pace with U.S. Treasuries. The underperformance for these areas, which marked another reversal from 2024, reflected an uptick in investor risk aversion.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted, primarily as a result of an overweight in U.S. mid caps and an underweight in the international markets. The fund also lost some relative performance by being underweight in bonds versus stocks, but we believe this element of our positioning is consistent with the fund's longer-term investment horizon. On the other hand, fund benefited from its underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks and corresponding overweight in defensive equities. The latter category performed as intended, providing a measure of ballast against volatility in the broader market. The real assets portfolio also helped results thanks to gains for natural resource and infrastructure stocks. This segment of the fund is designed to act as an offset to concerns against rising inflation, and it proved effective in that regard over the first three months of 2025.

Underlying manager results also detracted, with the weakest relative performance occurring in the domestic mid-cap and international categories.

Portfolio changes

At the close of the quarter, U.S. trade policy remained a significant wild card for the economy and financial markets. Our response to the uncertainty was to maintain a steady, balanced approach with small changes on the margin but no significant shifts. Instead of trying to be reactive to headlines, we strive to maintain a balanced approach. Balanced, in this case, isn't a traditional 60/40 stock/bond allocation. We instead look to keep the portfolios positioned for a broad range of outcomes for market drivers, such as inflation, growth, currency movements, and political developments. As always, we maintain a steady focus on the pillars of our investment process: valuations, fundamentals, and diversification. We believe the fund is well positioned to navigate the current environment through our flexibility to adjust the portfolio based on evolving conditions and capitalize on opportunities created by market volatility.

Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2030 Lifetime Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The first quarter marked a reversal of the trends in place during 2024, with a poor showing for U.S. growth stocks but stronger returns for most other asset categories.
  • The Multimanager 2030 Lifetime Portfolio fund produced a positive total return and outpaced the benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to results, but the relative performance of the underlying managers detracted.

Market review and outlook

Uncertainty regarding U.S. tariffs led to a wide range of returns across the world financial markets in the first quarter. U.S. equities were one of the worst performing asset categories on concerns that Washington's trade policies could fuel resurgent inflation and depress economic growth. Additionally, worries about reduced investment in AI-related infrastructure caused a sell-off in U.S. technology stocks. Although the U.S. growth style was hit hard by these developments, the value category posted a gain as investors sought opportunities in areas that had underperformed in 2024. Developed- and emerging-market stocks also recorded positive returns at a time of U.S. dollar weakness and positive sentiment on the outlook for European fiscal spending.

In the fixed-income market, higher-quality and more interest rate-sensitive assets delivered solid gains at a time of falling inflation and renewed hopes that the U.S. Federal Reserve would ease policy later in the year. The credit sectors (including investment-grade corporates, high-yield bonds, bank loans, and the emerging markets) produced positive returns but didn't keep pace with U.S. Treasuries. The underperformance for these areas, which marked another reversal from 2024, reflected an uptick in investor risk aversion.

Contributors and detractors

During the past two years, our diversified approach didn't have the anticipated benefits due to the high concentration of returns in a very narrow group of U.S. technology stocks. This trend changed in the first quarter as shifting headlines prompted a rotation toward other areas of the financial markets. We believe the fund's outperformance in this shifting environment helps illustrate the benefits of our emphasis on using a broadly diversified and long-term approach, rather than one that tries to chase short-term performance.

Asset allocation contributed to performance in the first quarter. The fund benefited from its underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks and a corresponding overweight in defensive equities. The real assets portfolio also helped results thanks to gains for natural resource and infrastructure stocks. This segment of the fund is designed to act as an offset to concerns against rising inflation, and it proved effective in that regard over the first three months of 2025. On the other hand, an underweight in the international markets detracted given the category's meaningful outperformance. The fund also lost some relative performance from an underweight in bonds versus stocks. Underlying manager results detracted, with the weakest relative performance occurring on the fixed-income side.

Portfolio changes

At the close of the quarter, U.S. trade policy remained a significant wild card for the economy and financial markets. Our response to the uncertainty was to maintain a steady, balanced approach with small changes on the margin but no significant shifts. Instead of trying to be reactive to headlines, we strive to maintain a balanced approach. Balanced, in this case, isn't a traditional 60/40 stock/bond allocation. We instead look to keep the portfolios positioned for a broad range of outcomes for market drivers, such as inflation, growth, currency movements, and political developments. As always, we maintain a steady focus on the pillars of our investment process: valuations, fundamentals, and diversification. We believe the fund is well positioned to navigate the current environment through our flexibility to adjust the portfolio based on evolving conditions and capitalize on opportunities created by market volatility.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of a loss.

data.title: Aurora Innovation: An Intriguing Long Haul Risk Capital Stock
data.description:
Thesis: Owing to the disruptive nature of its autonomous "Driver" platform, AUR is an intriguing buy. However, given the high valuation and opaque future, investors should consider restricting investments to risk capital.

Autonomous vehicles are likely the future, and while much of the focus has been on consumer-grade vehicles and robotaxis, the potential for autonomous systems to revolutionize the shipping industry shouldn’t be ignored. Shipping is a major cost for many companies and labor, in particular, can be both hard to find and expensive. Autonomous semi trucks could deliver huge cost savings and address labor shortages.

One company that’s worth keeping an eye on is Aurora Innovation (NASDAQ:AUR), which was founded in 2017 by industry heavy-weights Chris Urmson, who previously served as chief technology officer of what would become Alphabet’s Waymo, Sterling Anderson, once the head of Tesla’s Autopilot (NYSE:TSLA), and also Drew Bagnell, the former lead of Uber's autonomy and perception team.

That said, Aurora Innovation currently has a market cap north of $10 billion despite producing no revenues. While it’s easy to balk at such a high valuation for a company that’s still firmly in the R&D stage, the upside for AUR and its potential to disrupt large, highly lucrative markets make it an interesting “risk capital” stock.

Risk capital is the key word, however, and the risk of the company’s products never panning out or business mistakes sinking even good products can’t be ignored. Still, the market for self-driving semi trucks will be massive, and the cost savings of autonomous semis could measure in the many billions of dollars per year.

AUR’s Systems Have Hit the Road

AUR has already tested driverless semi trucks on a route running from Houston to Dallas. So far, early feedback suggests that the system is safe and working.

That said, after just three weeks of autonomous drivers hitting the road, AUR’s manufacturing partner Paccar requested that drivers be put back behind the wheel. Aurora Innovation has obliged for now, but suggested that the step was unnecessary, with CEO Chris Urmson pointing out:

“We are confident this is not required to operate the truck safely based on the exhaustive testing (covering nearly 10,000 requirements and 2.7 million tests) and analysis that populates our safety case. Paccar is a longtime partner and, after much consideration, we respected their request.”

That said, so far, Paccar has claimed that the reason is due to new prototype parts in the test vehicles. As far as I have seen, Paccar has not elaborated. For now, I'll give Paccar's statement the benefit of the doubt.

The Market Size for Self-Driving Semis is Hard to Ignore

Shipping via semi trucks is crucial in the United States and many other markets. By weight, semis moved over 72% of America’s freight in 2022. As of 2023, America’s domestic truck tonnage shipped topped 11 billion tons of freight transported (primary shipments only) (ibid). In 2022, single-unit (2-axle, +6-tires) and combination trucks registered in 2022 represented roughly 5% of all motor vehicles registered, totaling 14.33 million units (ibid).

Globally, the freight market is estimated to be worth $2.2 trillion and is projected to hit $3.4 trillion by 2030. In the United States alone, semi-truck shipping was worth $987 billion in 2023 (per the ATA data cited in the previous paragraph).

Aurora Innovation has also estimated the truck industry’s size, and the numbers (see below) are a bit different. Either way, truck driving is likely a trillion-dollar industry.

As for truck driving AI software, estimates conclude that the market could be worth $14.4 billion by 2030.

Aurora Valuation

Pinning down an evaluation for a company that’s not producing revenues can be rather speculative. I believe in looking at the size of the market and value of the product to guesstimate a company’s value. The market is obviously massive, and autonomous semis solve key pain points, including the labor shortage and restrictions on how many hours a driver can drive.

Long Term Benefits Could Be Tremendous

Will shipping companies embrace autonomous semi trucks? Absolutely, assuming the trucks prove safe and reliable, and government approval is obtained. As of 2024, labor accounted for roughly 30% of fleet operating expenses. Autonomous semis could reduce costs substantially.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics notes that there are 2,211,300 people employed as truck drivers. The average wage, meanwhile, has reached $57,440. With some rough napkin math, we can multiply 2,211,000 by $57,440 to consider how much is spent on labor per year.

2,211,000 X $57,440 = $126,999,840,000

In other words, the potential for cost savings is immense. Autonomous semis will almost certainly cost more than traditional semi trucks, but the long-term savings will likely pay off. AUR’s technologies could provide a variety of other benefits and massively increase efficiency. The graphic below points out some of the key benefits.

Partnerships in the Future?

General Motors recently hired one of AUR’s key executives, Sterling Anderson, as its Chief Product Officer. Some speculate that this could hint at closer ties and perhaps even an acquisition. That’s highly speculative, but something to keep an eye on, and if GM makes a move, AUR’s shares could surge.

Aurora Innovation Risks

As a speculative stock, risks abound, and unfortunately, even one mishap could cause immense damage. Below, I’ve highlighted some key concerns, but this list is far from exhaustive.

Liabilities Will Always Be High

It’s a safe bet that if one of AUR’s trucks causes an accident, the company will be on the hook for huge liabilities, and a lawsuit would probably be inevitable. The company would also face increased scrutiny, and governments might push for more onerous oversight and regulations. Timelines to allow more autonomous vehicles on the road would likely be pushed back.

This could spell serious trouble given that AUR isn’t even producing revenue and is spending lots of cash. As of the last reporting, AUR had $1,159 in cash and short-term investments. However, the last reported operating income totaled -$804 million. Fortunately, AUR has only $286 million in liabilities.

Regulatory Approval Presents Hurdles

So far, governments have used a relatively heavy-handed approach to autonomous cars, and timelines remain vague. If governments drag their feet long enough, it could sink AUR or at least cause a lot of damage.

Disgruntled Workers

For better or worse, autonomy will eliminate many truck driver jobs. Drivers and organizations like the Teamsters may fight back with zeal. Replacing jobs with AI may also draw criticism from the wider society.

Ground Up Integrated Systems Probably Represent the Future

Aurora Innovation’s technologies currently focus on retrofitting existing semi trucks. The ability to retrofit semis should initially allow the company to scale relatively quickly. However, in the long run, I believe that semi trucks with the necessary autonomous sensors and systems built in will perform better and will eventually be more affordable.

Daimler Truck is currently working on semi trucks with autonomous systems already baked in. This creates a competitive risk for Aurora Innovation. However, I believe that AUR’s tech stack can be readapted to be built into new semi trucks. There may be opportunities to adapt AUR’s technologies to consumer vehicles as well.

The Tech Could Simply Fail or Fall Short of the Competition

While Aurora Innovation’s technology development and performance have been promising, there’s certainly no guarantee that they will succeed. Even if AUR does achieve its technological goals, competition might be able to overwhelm them. For example, if Tesla adopts a Vision-only approach for its semitrucks and the system performs well, Tesla may be able to deliver similar outcomes at a lower price.

Takeaway: Upside is High, But So Too Are the Risks

It’s worth mentioning again: AUR is a speculative bet. Most investors should probably stick to risk capital when investing in this company. Right now, AUR insiders control more than 30% of shares, suggesting a high degree of faith in the company. Much of the rest is held by institutions. If AUR can deliver on its technologies, the company might cement itself as one of the behemoth tech companies as share prices soar.

data.title: Tariff Bombshell Fizzles, Nvidia Still Shines
data.description:
Wall Street faced a whirlwind of news Thursday - illegal tariffs! legal again! - but mostly shrugged, deciding that judicial drama and AI gold rushes cancel each other out, at least until tomorrow’s headline roulette spins again.

Level Change 5/29/25 (%)

+0.3 Dow

+0.4 Nasdaq

+0.2 Nasdaq 100

+0.4 S&P 500

+0.3 S&P 400

+0.4 S&P 600

Stocks wandered modestly higher in a session that offered more confusion than conviction.

The day’s main plot twist - President Trump’s signature tariffs declared illegal - was immediately spun into a bureaucratic blur, then promptly paused by a federal appeals court. So yes, tariffs are illegal, but also still legal, and possibly headed to the Supreme Court, where justices can weigh in on whether emergency powers include taxing toasters from Toronto.

The original ruling said Trump had overreached by invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to slap duties on nearly every country. The White House called the ruling “rife with legal error,” appealed, and secured a stay just before the market close. As of now, the tariffs remain in effect, and traders remain unsure what just happened. Call them puzzled, not panicked.

That might be because Nvidia (NVDA) offered something better to focus on.

The AI bellwether (NVDA +3.3%) delivered strong results that didn’t just clear a high bar, they brought their own. Concerns over China's export losses were drowned out by surging demand for inference computing and the full-speed launch of its new Blackwell rack system. CEO Jensen Huang declared that “every nation now sees AI as core to the next industrial revolution,” which is probably true, though some are eyeing it more like the next oil, with accompanying turf wars.

Elsewhere on this laid-back Thursday, Fed Chair Powell dropped by the White House for what the Fed described as a neutral, data-driven chat. Trump restated that the Fed should cut rates. Powell nodded politely and restated the importance of “careful, objective, nonpolitical analysis” - code for “you run your show, we’ll run the numbers.”

Goolsbee of the Chicago Fed dangled the idea that avoiding tariffs might clear a path for rate cuts. But FOMC minutes tilted hawkish, with slowdown anxieties top of mind. And don’t lose sight of this: Even if the biggest tariffs vanish, what’s left would still be the steepest since 1969, keeping pressure on growth.

So we’re left where we’ve been: staring at a market balanced between policy purgatory and AI euphoria. Nvidia lit the path forward, but Washington lit a legal fuse. Watch which burns faster.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Flexsteel's Lower Share Price Is Not Enough Given The Consumer Discretionary Cycle Risk
data.description:
Flexsteel (NASDAQ:FLXS) reported earnings (3Q25 on their fiscal year) last month, during the heat of the tariff scare. The company's results were generally good in terms of growth and profitability, maybe negatively impacted by a large lease cancellation.

In terms of tariffs, the company will see an impact from the 10% tariff on Vietnamese production (50% of their sales). More importantly, the company's management showed concern about the effect of tariffs on the US economy. They commented on a significant slowdown in April.

Flexsteel's price has come down 50% since my last article on the name (in February 2025). The name now trades at about 8/9x expected FY25 earnings, with a combined dividend and stock buyback return yield of about 3.5%. This multiple seems correct in principle, but we also need to account for cyclicality. The 2022/24 period was very positive for Flexsteel, but that cycle will probably end this year, with the challenges in the US economy being the driver. For that reason, I prefer to wait for lower prices that reflect a more attractive cyclical entry point.

3Q25 results, positive with some notes

3Q25 results were good, in line with the company's performance for the past two years. Revenues were up, mainly coming from new products (already 50% of current sales according to the call), and the expansion in current and new retailer partners.

The increase in revenues and sales helps leverage the company's relatively fixed cost structure in terms of SG&A and manufacturing, leading to an increase in adjusted operating income.

So far, that would be a relatively unimportant quarter, with no indications of a cyclical challenge, and continued market share gains.

Small note on lease impairment

The only callout would be the impairment of ROU assets of $14 million (leading to an operating loss of $5 million), because of the recognition that a lease in Mexicali, Mexico, will not be recovered. This was a factory that Flexsteel leased in 2022 for higher production, which was then rented to sublessors. These subleases are harder to come by because of lower investment in Mexico, leading to impairment.

Although the impairment is nonrecurring and will actually benefit the earnings of the company going forward (it will avoid the D&A charge from this ROU asset), the cash cost will be felt, quarter after quarter. When a lease is impaired, only the asset side is modified, with the lease-debt side remaining. The only difference is that the whole cost of the lease is recognized as a loss in a single quarter, instead of spreading it through D&A. That means in the future FLXS earnings will show a lower conversion to FCF (the lease will not show up in earnings but will require cash payments).

Tariffs

The company commented during the call that its exposure to the tariffs is actually moderate. FLXS sources 55% of its products from Vietnam (10% tariffs) and 40% from Mexico (no tariffs under USMCA). This structure is actually an advantage, because other competitors probably import much more from China or Vietnam, compared to the Mexico exposure.

The company expects to offset part of the impact from Vietnam tariffs via a surcharge, but did not make changes to long-term margins.

However, management was very negative about the health of the value chain. Demand from retailers had been positive through March, but during April orders fell significantly, and some previous orders were cancelled.

The company has a backlog of about 3 months ($80 million over about $400 million in sales), and warned that without order replenishment, it would fall.

The comments were made in late April, when the US-China agreement had not yet been reached. Retailers might be less inactive now. Further, if consumer demand persists, retailers will need to continue sourcing products, and Flexsteel is in a good position to supply them, especially from its Mexican operations.

Still, the outlook is very uncertain for FY26.

Valuation and consumer cycle

Flexsteel's stock price has fallen 50% since February. Most of the fall occurred before the tariff announcements, probably driven by fears of a challenging consumer cycle upcoming.

We must understand that, like most consumer-discretionary exposed companies, Flexsteel's earnings are cyclical.

This means that when valuing Flexsteel, we cannot blindly assume that it will keep growing into eternity without considering the health of the US consumer. The company might do very well in product introduction, but a big part of its demand comes from a healthy market environment.

Taking that into account, the company's valuation remains high. Flexsteel currently trades at a market cap of $170 million. It expects to generate adjusted operating income (adjusted for the lease impairment and property sales) of about $28 million, or $21 million in net income after taxes (no debt). That is a 'low' multiple of only 8.5x earnings. In addition, the name pays a dividend of $3 million and buys back about the same amount in shares, generating a return yield of about 3.5%.

However, on a cyclical basis, the company's operating income average is probably closer to $15 million, or $10 million after taxes. That implies that the name trades at a 17x multiple of that cycle.

We never know when the consumer cycle will turn, but the current signs are particularly worrying. Not only was the cycle already challenged, but the tariff impact on inflation and employment will add weight.

For that reason, I remain skeptical of Flexsteel's mid-term attractiveness at current prices. I am still interested in the execution of its management team, which seems to have gained share in this last cycle. However, for the time being, I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Pebblebrook Hotel Trust Is Undervalued, I'm Picking Up An 8.7% Dividend Yield With The Preferreds
data.description:
Pebblebrook Hotel Trust (NYSE:PEB) is down 32% year-to-date, compounding a discount to net asset value ("NAV") which dramatically widened in 2024. The internally managed hotel REIT reported a GAAP tangible book value of $2.63 billion, around $22.28 per share, as of the end of its fiscal 2025 first quarter. PEB is currently swapping hands for $9.30 per share, a roughly 60% discount to GAAP book value per share, or for 40 cents on the dollar. There wasn't always a discount, with PEB trading at a premium pre-pandemic. The inversion of this was sparked by the REIT's exposure to the San Francisco lodging market. The vacancy rate in the city reached 29% in April, a 310 basis points increase versus its year-ago comp to maintain its position as the highest metro area office vacancy in the USA, far above the national average of 19.7%. These urban locations depend more heavily on business travellers for occupancy. Fewer business trips on the back of the post-pandemic remote working zeitgeist is driving weakness. This forms the core of the bear case against PEB and a core risk for its investors.

The sustained dip in book value has not been helpful, with the boom in the Fed funds rate from 2022 driving elevated interest expenses that have kept the REIT's dividend to its common shareholders at the same peppercorn rate it was cut to at the onset of the pandemic. PEB last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.01 per share, unchanged from its prior distribution and $0.04 per share annualized for a 0.44% dividend yield. However, the current discount is far too dramatic, providing the baseline for a near-term partial tightening. I don't expect the discount to be fully closed, but a return to a trading range of around $13 per share to $14 per share most reflects PEB's financials and guidance.

Dual Earnings Beats, LaPlaya Comes Back Online, Balance Sheet Health, And The Preferreds

PEB generated first quarter revenue of $320.27 million, up 2% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $8.6 million on consensus estimates. This came on the back of same-property total RevPAR which grew by 2.1% over its year-ago quarter. This growth would have been 6% if Los Angeles hotels were excluded. These have seen softer demand as a result of the forest fires earlier this year. Adjusted FFO came in at $18.7 million, around $0.16 per share, outperforming consensus by 4 cents per share. Critically, PEB expects growth in 2025 to be significant on a year-over-year basis as its LaPlaya Beach Resort & Club in Naples, Florida fully comes back online after being damaged by Hurricanes Helene and Milton. The remaining 20 first-floor guestrooms should be reopened by the end of June, and the asset should contribute an additional $13 million to $15 million in hotel EBITDA through the remainder of 2025.

The REIT is guiding for second-quarter adjusted FFO to at a minimum come in at $68.5 million, around $0.57 per share. Full year FFO is expected to be in the range of $170 million to $190.5 million, around $1.42 to $1.59 on a per share basis. PEB's multiple against the midpoint of this adjusted FFO range of 6.17x is far too low for a REIT set to generate FFO of $1.50 per share at its midpoint for 2025. The REIT does not pay a substantial dividend, with its yield 91% lower than its sector median. PEB generated free cash flow of $50.34 million during the quarter, down sequentially but at a level where the company more than comprehensively covered the aggregate quarterly coupon payment to its preferred shareholders.

This coverage provides substantial confidence for holders of the four outstanding preferreds, the 6.375% Series I Preferreds (NYSE:PEB.PR.E), 6.30% Series J Preferreds (NYSE:PEB.PR.F), 6.375% Series G Preferreds (NYSE:PEB.PR.G), and 5.70% Series H Preferreds (NYSE:PEB.PR.H). PEB held $218.1 million in cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash as of the end of the first quarter. The REIT also faces no debt maturities until December 2026, providing ample time for expected rate cuts to feed through to future efforts to refinance its debt. Total debt stood at $2.26 billion as of the end of the first quarter, held at a weighted average interest rate of 4.2%.

The December debt maturity next year is significant at $750 million, and the REIT will need to refinance this against its current cash position. But the year-to-date dip looks exaggerated against its current multiple to FFO and future estimates for FFO. The preferreds look especially attractive, with the Series H preferreds now trading for an 8.7% yield on cost and for 66 cents on the dollar. I'm buying at this level.

data.title: Tracking Ray Dalio's Bridgewater Associates 13F Portfolio - Q1 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Bridgewater Associates’ 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F Form filed on 05/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking Ray Dalio’s Bridgewater Associates 13F Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our previous update for the fund’s moves during Q4 2024.

Assets Under Management is at around $155B. The SPDR Bridgewater ALL Weather ETF (NASDAQ:ALLW) is a way to get exposure to Bridgewater for the retail audience. The 13F portfolio is less than ~15% of their total AUM. This quarter, the 13F portfolio value decreased from ~$21.81B to ~$21.55B. The holdings are diversified with recent reports showing around 1000 different stakes. Around 39 of them are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the 13F portfolio) and they are the focus of this article. The top three individual stock positions are at ~7% while the top five holdings are close to ~10% of the 13F assets: Alibaba Group Holdings, Alphabet, Booking Holdings, Nvidia, and Microsoft.

Note 1: The firm uses asset class diversification among uncorrelated positions to achieve absolute returns. As such the stakes can be on or against debt, equity, and other markets around the world. To learn more about their unique investment philosophy, check out Ray Dalio’s books.

Note 2: Ray Dalio is a Sinophile whose fascination for China goes back to 1984.

Stake Increases:

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA): BABA is now the largest position in the portfolio at 3.47%. It was primarily built this quarter at prices between ~$80 and ~$148. The stock currently trades at ~$117.

Booking Holdings (BKNG): The top-three 1.43% of the portfolio position was built this quarter at prices between ~$4279 and ~$5282. The stock is now well above that range at ~$5502.

Microsoft Corp (MSFT): MSFT is a 1.41% of the portfolio position that saw a ~90% stake increase during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$388 and ~$452. The position was sold down by ~20% in the next quarter at prices between ~$394 and ~$467. The last quarter saw a ~23% selling at prices between ~$406 and ~$455 while this quarter there was a ~21% increase at prices between ~$367 and ~$448. The stock is now at ~$459.

Salesforce Inc. (CRM): CRM is a 2.30% of the portfolio position that saw a stake doubling this quarter at prices between ~$259 and ~$367. The stock currently trades near the bottom of that range at ~$267.

Fiserv Inc. (FI): The 1.19% FI stake saw a ~80% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$200 and ~$239. The last quarter also saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between ~$179 and ~$223. The stock is now well below those ranges at ~$160.

Amazon.com (AMZN): The 1.17% of the portfolio AMZN position was established during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$145 and ~$180. The next quarter saw a ~150% stake increase at prices between ~$174 and ~$198. There was a ~47% selling during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$161 and ~$200. That was followed by a one-third reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$181 and ~$233. There was a ~44% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$184 and ~$243. The stock currently trades at ~$206.

Wells Fargo (WFC): WFC is a 1.14% of the portfolio position that saw a ~75% stake increase this quarter at prices between $65.52 and $81.50. The stock is now at $74.51.

Visa Inc. (V): The 1.13% Visa stake saw a ~18% increase during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$207 and ~$231. Q3 2023 saw a ~16% trimming while in the next quarter there was a ~28% stake increase at prices between ~$229 and ~$262. The stake was decreased by 13% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$257 and ~$290. The next quarter saw another ~15% selling at prices between ~$262 and ~$280. Q3 2024 saw another ~25% reduction at prices between ~$253 and ~$291. This quarter saw a ~14% stake increase at prices between ~$304 and ~$367. The stock is now at ~$362.

PayPal Holdings (PYPL): PYPL is now a 1.09% of the portfolio position. The stake was almost doubled during the last quarter at prices between ~$77 and ~$91. That was followed by a ~50% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$63 and ~$93. The stock is now at ~$71.

Adobe Inc. (ADBE): The ~1% ADBE position saw a ~150% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$375 and ~$466. The stock currently trades at ~$413.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD): AMD is a ~1% stake established during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$135 and ~$211. There was a ~62% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$144 and ~$183. Q3 2024 saw a one-third stake increase at prices between ~$129 and ~$184. There was a ~23% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$119 and ~$173. This quarter also saw a similar increase at prices between ~$95 and ~$132. The stock is now at ~$113.

Apple Inc. (AAPL): The ~1% AAPL stake was more than doubled during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$207 and ~$234. The last quarter saw a ~40% selling at prices between ~$221 and ~$259 while this quarter there was a ~52% increase at prices between ~$208 and ~$250. The stock is now at ~$200.

Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM): QCOM is a 0.78% of the portfolio position built during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$155 and ~$209. There was a ~13% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$150 and ~$177. That was followed by a ~20% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$148 and ~$177. The stock currently trades at ~$148.

Broadcom (AVGO): AVGO is a 0.73% of the portfolio position built during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$136 and ~$178. There was a ~31% reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$159 and ~$249 while in this quarter there was a ~43% increase at prices between ~$161 and ~$250. The stock is now at ~$242.

Altria Group (MO), Baidu Inc. (BIDU), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Comcast Corp (CMCSA), Expedia Group (EXPE), Fortinet Inc. (FTNT), GE Vernova (GEV), MetLife (MET), Citigroup (C), Palo Alto Networks (PANW), PDD Holdings (PDD), and ServiceNow (NOW): These stakes were increased this quarter.

Stake Decreases:

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG): GOOG is currently the second largest 13F individual stock position at 2.18% of the portfolio. It saw a ~210% stake increase during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$87 and ~$109. There was a ~15% trimming in the next quarter and that was followed by a ~20% reduction during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$117 and ~$139. Q4 2023 saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$123 and ~$143. The position was increased by 163% during Q1 2024 at prices between~ $133 and $~155. There was a ~15% trimming during the next quarter at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. That was followed by a ~17% selling during the last quarter at prices between $162.88 and $198.16. This quarter also saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$152 and ~$209. The stock currently trades at ~$173.

Nvidia Corp. (NVDA): The top five 1.43% NVDA position was increased by 162% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$48 and ~$95. The stake was established in Q4 2023 at prices between ~$40 and ~$51. Q3 2024 saw a ~28% selling at prices between $98.90 and $134.90. That was followed by a ~26% reduction during the last quarter at prices between $116.99 and $148.87. This quarter also saw a ~19% selling at prices between ~$104 and ~$153. The stock currently trades at ~$139.

Meta Platforms (META): META is a 1.14% of the portfolio stake that was increased by 49% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$344 and ~$512. Q3 2024 saw a ~17% reduction at prices between ~$453 and ~$568. That was followed by a ~23% selling during the last quarter at prices between $553.63 and $632.17. This quarter also saw a ~32% reduction at prices between ~$553 and ~$741. The stock is now at ~$645.

Vistra Corp (VST): The 0.89% VST stake saw a ~135% increase during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$69 and ~$119. The last quarter saw a ~60% further increase at prices between ~$116 and ~$166. The stock is now at ~$160. There was a ~8% trimming this quarter.

Constellation Energy (CEG): The 0.88% CEG position saw a ~23% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$224 and ~$285. The stock currently trades at ~$303. This quarter saw marginal trimming.

Lam Research (LRCX): The 0.66% LRCX stake was primarily built during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$71 and ~$109. This quarter saw a ~23% selling at prices between ~$70 and ~$92. The stock currently trades at ~$84.

McKesson Corp (MCK): MCK is a 0.49% stake that saw a one-third increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$482 and ~$628. This quarter saw a ~53% selling at prices between ~$566 and ~$679. The stock currently trades at ~$713.

Walmart Stores (WMT): The WMT position was purchased during the four quarters through Q2 2021 at prices between ~$40 and ~$51. Q4 2021 saw the stake sold down by ~45% at prices between ~$45 and ~$50. H1 2022 had seen a two-thirds increase at prices between ~$39 and ~$53. The four quarters through Q2 2023 saw a ~48% selling at prices between ~$41 and ~$52. There was a ~55% reduction during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$67 and ~$81. The last quarter saw a one-third selling at prices between ~$79 and ~$96. This quarter saw the position almost sold out at prices between ~$84 and ~$105. The stock is now at ~$97.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 3-for-1 stock split in February 2024.

AppLovin Corp (APP), AT&T Inc. (T), HCA Healthcare (HCA), Merck Inc. (MRK), and The Cigna Group (CI): These stakes were reduced during the quarter.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Bridgewater Associates’ 13F holdings during Q1 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Bridgewater Associates’ 13F filings for Q4 2024 and Q1 2025.

data.title: COWG: High Tech, High Momentum, High Returns, High Valuation
data.description:
US large-cap growth has rebounded into positive territory year-to-date. While mega-cap tech took it on the chin to begin the year, a May rebirth among the Magnificent Seven stocks has helped send both the Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) and the S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG) back to black for 2025.

One new fund, the Pacer US Large Cap Cash Cows Growth Leaders ETF (NASDAQ:COWG) has tracked US large-cap growth since its inception less than three years ago. The fund received a boost, including an influx of investor flows, following the US election in 2024. I noticed big volume over the past handful of months, but I have a hold rating on the product.

The valuation is not all that compelling, while its risky holdings make COWG susceptible to a pullback as we approach mid-year. I’ll call out key price levels to monitor at the conclusion of the article.

COWG Very Strong Post-Election, Returns On Pace with SPYG

According to the issuer, COWG is a strategy-driven ETF that aims to identify top growth companies in the Russell 1000 by screening for above-average free cash flow margins. Free cash flow is the cash remaining after a company has paid expenses, interest, taxes, and long-term investments. It is the source from which dividends are paid. Companies that have the highest free cash flow margin over time may produce higher current and future sales and earnings growth.

Ranked No. 3 out of 28 in its Sub Class, COWG is a small ETF with just $1.8 billion in assets under management as of May 28, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 49 basis points, which is about 0.4 percentage points above what you can get for a standard US growth fund. Moreover, the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 31 basis points, which is about a full percentage point below that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been extremely strong lately, though, as evidenced by a pristine A+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. COWG has benefited from a superb rebound in the US equity momentum factor, and you’ll see later that some of its biggest components are momentum darlings.

But COWG sports just average risk grades, and we’ve already seen this year that steep corrections can happen on a moment’s notice. From a liquidity perspective, there aren’t many issues, considering its average daily volume of more than 700,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of less than two basis points, per Pacer ETFs.

Looking closer at the portfolio, what makes me uneasy with COWG today is the valuation. Now trading at nearly 25x earnings, the PEG ratio is almost three, given the long-term earnings growth rate of only 9%. Performance depends on key themes playing out, including AI and cybersecurity. While I am bullish on those fronts, and I appreciate the profitability and free cash flow focuses, the ETF is not a bargain today.

COWG: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Increasing the product’s risk is the portfolio breakdown. More than 60% of COWG is classified as being in the Information Technology sector, with another 13% in Health Care. That’s about three-quarters of the allocation in just two growth-oriented areas. So, there’s a lack of industry diversification.

COWG: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal point of view, I must concede that bullish winds are blowing. We typically see US large-cap growth perform quite well now through July. Though COWG includes a significant chunk of mid-cap US growth, we can still use SPYG calendar trends as a decent proxy for how the ETF may perform if seasonal price action is any clue.

US Growth Tends to Perform Well Through July-August

For COWG’s holdings specifically, I’d call out that we’ll get a handful of Annual General Meeting events now through the middle of June. Five of its top 10 company holdings have shareholder events through June 18, so that could stir some share-price volatility before the Q2 earnings season gets underway.

Shareholder Meetings on Tap

The Technical Take

From a technical perspective, COWG looks solid. Notice in the chart below that we’ve seen a massive volume increase just since January. The fund performed incredibly well after the election, and that alpha period may have drawn in new money. Shares peaked, though, in mid-February, and then went on to plunge about 30% to the April low, sharply underperforming the S&P 500.

I like what I see in the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph, but key price resistance remains in the $35 to $36 zone, so we’ll see if the bulls can bring the growth ETF through that level during this typically strong seasonal stretch. I do see a downside gap around the $33 mark that could be tagged in the weeks ahead, but with a high amount of volume by price down to $32, we might just be in an ongoing congestion zone.

Bigger picture, the long-term 200-day moving average remains positively sloped, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

COWG: Strong Uptrend, Big Volume YTD, $36 Resistance, $33 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on COWG. The growth-oriented US equity fund that seems to have a bent to the momentum factor has a premium valuation, while its chart is constructive heading into the summer.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Makes A Comeback
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 4.8 percentage points to 32.9%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 16th time in 17 weeks, and is above 30% for only the eighth time this year.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 0.3 percentage points to 25.2%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 45th time in 47 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 5.1 percentage points to 41.9%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 26th time in 28 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 10.0 percentage points to –9.0%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 21st time in 23 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members which factor is most influencing their six-month outlook for stocks.

Here is how they responded:

  • Tariffs, the economy, and/or inflation: 63.7%
  • Corporate earnings: 10.7%
  • Valuations: 10.4%
  • Monetary policy/interest rates: 8.9%
  • Other: 6.3%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 32.9%, down 4.8 percentage points
  • Neutral: 25.2%, down 0.3 percentage points
  • Bearish: 41.9%, up 5.1 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: EQB Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by EQB Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.
